original_abstract:
Specific electrolyte effects have remained a fundamental scientific challenge since the 1880s, beginning with Franz Hofmeister’s pioneering observations on salt-induced protein precipitation. Far from being anomalies, these effects are ubiquitous across chemistry and biology, yet they remain unexplained by conventional electrostatic frameworks such as Debye-Hückel or DLVO theory. Over the last few decades, research has shifted toward incorporating quantum mechanical dispersion forces and associated hydration effects into theoretical models. Concurrently, Collins’ "Law of Matching Water Affinities" (LMWA) has provided a robust phenomenological framework that qualitatively organizes ion-ion and ion-surface interactions. 

While both approaches attempt to bridge the gaps in classical theory, they often appear to be in conflict. Although the necessity of including quantum dispersion forces is widely accepted, many existing models fail to capture the specific chemical nature—such as the kosmotropic or chaotropic character—of the interacting species. The LMWA succeeds precisely where these models falter. In this paper, we propose a pathway to reconcile these two seemingly opposing perspectives. Furthermore, we address broader complexities, including the influence of dissolved gases on "hydrophobic" interactions and the shifting nature of water structure at varying temperatures. These phenomena suggest that a complete understanding requires a fundamental rebuilding of our theoretical foundations.
humanized_abstract:
Since the 1880s, with the pioneering work of Franz Hofmeister in relation to the phenomenon of protein precipitation upon addition of salts, the specific electrolyte effects have remained an open scientific issue. These effects are obviously considered as exceptions in relation to the common electrostatic approaches developed in chemistry and biology, from Debye-Hückel to DLVO theories. However, during the last few decades, the state-of-the-art has clearly evolved toward the inclusion of quantum mechanical dispersion interactions and hydration effects into the theoretical frameworks, aiming at an improved understanding of the ion pairing and ion–surface interactions in solutions. It is remarkable that the LFCA, which is the most widely used framework to describe electrolyte effects at the interfaces, is simply a phenomenological extension of Collins’ Law of Matching Water Affinities (LMWA) that elegantly organizes ion–ion and ion–surface interactions.

While many approaches are being proposed in order to fill the gaps of classical theory (often resulting in an opposition between them), the necessity of including the quantum dispersion forces in the interaction models is today widely admitted, although, in many cases, the corresponding models do not take into account the specific chemical character (for instance kosmotropic or chaotropic) of the molecules involved. The Local Mean-Value Approach (LMWA) is more successful in doing so. This paper shows how these two opposed approaches may be linked and how they have to be taken into account in order to solve also other important problems of relevance in solution chemistry, as for instance, the influence of dissolved gases on what is generally called the hydrophobic effect, and the changes occurring in the water structure as a function of temperature. This appears to mean that a large revision of present theories is needed.

document.output:
Since the 1880s, with the pioneering work of Franz Hofmeister in relation to the phenomenon of protein precipitation upon addition of salts, the specific electrolyte effects have remained an open scientific issue. These effects are obviously considered as exceptions in relation to the common electrostatic approaches developed in chemistry and biology, from Debye-Hückel to DLVO theories. However, during the last few decades, the state-of-the-art has clearly evolved toward the inclusion of quantum mechanical dispersion interactions and hydration effects into the theoretical frameworks, aiming at an improved understanding of the ion pairing and ion–surface interactions in solutions. It is remarkable that the LFCA, which is the most widely used framework to describe electrolyte effects at the interfaces, is simply a phenomenological extension of Collins’ Law of Matching Water Affinities (LMWA) that elegantly organizes ion–ion and ion–surface interactions.

While many approaches are being proposed in order to fill the gaps of classical theory (often resulting in an opposition between them), the necessity of including the quantum dispersion forces in the interaction models is today widely admitted, although, in many cases, the corresponding models do not take into account the specific chemical character (for instance kosmotropic or chaotropic) of the molecules involved. The Local Mean-Value Approach (LMWA) is more successful in doing so. This paper shows how these two opposed approaches may be linked and how they have to be taken into account in order to solve also other important problems of relevance in solution chemistry, as for instance, the influence of dissolved gases on what is generally called the hydrophobic effect, and the changes occurring in the water structure as a function of temperature. This appears to mean that a large revision of present theories is needed.
document.input:
Specific electrolyte effects have remained a fundamental scientific challenge since the 1880s, beginning with Franz Hofmeister’s pioneering observations on salt-induced protein precipitation. Far from being anomalies, these effects are ubiquitous across chemistry and biology, yet they remain unexplained by conventional electrostatic frameworks such as Debye-Hückel or DLVO theory. Over the last few decades, research has shifted toward incorporating quantum mechanical dispersion forces and associated hydration effects into theoretical models. Concurrently, Collins’ "Law of Matching Water Affinities" (LMWA) has provided a robust phenomenological framework that qualitatively organizes ion-ion and ion-surface interactions. 

While both approaches attempt to bridge the gaps in classical theory, they often appear to be in conflict. Although the necessity of including quantum dispersion forces is widely accepted, many existing models fail to capture the specific chemical nature—such as the kosmotropic or chaotropic character—of the interacting species. The LMWA succeeds precisely where these models falter. In this paper, we propose a pathway to reconcile these two seemingly opposing perspectives. Furthermore, we address broader complexities, including the influence of dissolved gases on "hydrophobic" interactions and the shifting nature of water structure at varying temperatures. These phenomena suggest that a complete understanding requires a fundamental rebuilding of our theoretical foundations.
